title: About
text: Hello! I am Seunghyeon Kang, an undergraduate researcher interested in bioinformatics and data science.

title: Omics: Functional Gene Annotation Analysis
text: This analysis explores functional gene annotations across four bacterial strain types: Mal, Dimeth, Chlorp, and Diaz. Protein annotations were obtained from OmicsBox and are compared to identify shared and unique functional categories across strains.

title: Omics: Functional Gene Annotation Analysis
text: This analysis explores functional gene annotations across four bacterial strain types: Mal, Dimeth, Chlorp, and Diaz. Protein annotations were obtained from OmicsBox and are compared to identify shared and unique functional categories across strains.

title: Omics: Functional Gene Annotation Analysis
text:
Setup

# Load all required packages
library(tidyverse)
library(here)


# Custom colorblind-friendly palette used throughout visualizations
colors_van_likes <- c(
  "#E69F00", # orange
  "#009E73", # bluish green
  "#F0E442", # yellow
  "#D55E00", # vermillion
  "#0072B2", # blue
  "#CC79A7"  # purple/pink
)

title: Omics: Functional Gene Annotation Analysis
text:
Data Cleaning

# Helper: read a list of OmicsBox .txt files and add strain metadata
read_omics_files <- function(files, strain_type) {
  files |>
    set_names() |>
    map_dfr(
      ~ read_tsv(.x, show_col_types = FALSE),
      .id = "file"
    ) |>
    mutate(
      Strain      = str_remove(file, "_omicsbox_table\\.txt$"),
      StrainType  = strain_type,
      Description = str_remove(Description, "^MULTISPECIES:\\s*")
    ) |>
    select(-file)
}


# --- Mal / Dimeth strains (already combined in MalDimeth.csv) ---
mal_dimeth_df <- read_csv(
  here("MalDimeth.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
) |>
  mutate(
    StrainType = case_when(
      str_detect(Strain, "^Dimeth") ~ "Dimeth",
      str_detect(Strain, "^Mal")   ~ "Mal",
      TRUE                          ~ "Other"
    ),
    Description = str_remove(Description, "^MULTISPECIES:\\s*")
  )

# --- Chlorp strains ---
chlorp_files <- c(
  "Chlorp2_omicsbox_table.txt",
  "Chlorp5_omicsbox_table.txt",
  "Chlorp7_omicsbox_table.txt",
  "Chlorp8_omicsbox_table.txt",
  "Chlorp10_omicsbox_table.txt",
  "Chlorp11_omicsbox_table.txt"
)

# --- Diaz strains ---
diaz_files <- c(
  "Diaz11_omicsbox_table.txt",
  "Diaz23_omicsbox_table.txt",
  "Diaz26_omicsbox_table.txt",
  "Diaz28_omicsbox_table.txt"
)

chlorp_df <- read_omics_files(chlorp_files, "Chlorp")
diaz_df   <- read_omics_files(diaz_files,   "Diaz")


# Merge all strain data into one dataframe and save clean version
df <- bind_rows(mal_dimeth_df, chlorp_df, diaz_df)

write_csv(df, here("data_clean", "MalChlorpDimethDiaz.csv"))

# Helper: categorize gene annotations into broad functional groups
categorize_function <- function(desc) {
  desc <- str_to_lower(desc)
  case_when(
    str_detect(
      desc,
      "transporter|permease|abc|symporter|receptor|efflux"
    ) ~ "Transport",
    str_detect(
      desc,
      "regulator|transcription|sigma|response regulator|kinase|phosphatase|sensor"
    ) ~ "Regulation",
    str_detect(
      desc,
      "dehydrogenase|oxidoreductase|synthase|ligase|transferase|hydrolase|metabolic|biosynthetic|catabolic"
    ) ~ "Metabolism",
    str_detect(
      desc,
      "stress|resistance|glutathione|repair|tellurite|osm|detox"
    ) ~ "Stress/Defense",
    str_detect(
      desc,
      "ribosomal|elongation factor|polymerase|trna ligase"
    ) ~ "Information Processing",
    TRUE ~ "Other"
  )
}

title: Omics: Functional Gene Annotation Analysis
text:
Visualizations

Functional gene categories by strain

# Count genes per functional category within each strain
df |>
  mutate(Category = categorize_function(Description)) |>
  count(Strain, Category) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = Strain, y = n, fill = Category)) +
  geom_col() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = colors_van_likes) +
  labs(
    title   = "Functional gene categories across strains",
    x       = "Strain",
    y       = "Number of annotated genes",
    fill    = "Functional category",
    caption = "Source: OmicsBox functional annotation tables"
  ) +
  theme_classic() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))







Figure 1: Stacked bar chart of annotated gene counts per strain, grouped by broad functional category.





Functional annotations were grouped into broad categories including metabolism, transport, regulation, stress/defense, and information processing. Transport- and metabolism-related genes were abundant across all Mal strains, with smaller differences among strains in stress/defense and regulatory genes.



Gene sharing within strain types

# Count how many strains within a type share each annotation
shared_genes <- df |>
  filter(!is.na(Description), Description != "") |>
  distinct(StrainType, Strain, Description) |>
  count(StrainType, Description, name = "n_strains") |>
  count(StrainType, n_strains)

ggplot(shared_genes, aes(x = factor(n_strains), y = n, fill = factor(n_strains))) +
  geom_col() +
  scale_fill_viridis_d() +
  facet_wrap(~ StrainType, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(
    title   = "Distribution of shared and unique gene annotations within strain types",
    x       = "Number of strains sharing a gene annotation",
    y       = "Number of gene annotations",
    fill    = "# of strains",
    caption = "Source: OmicsBox functional annotation tables"
  ) +
  theme_classic()







Figure 2: Distribution of how many strains within each strain type share a given gene annotation. High counts at x = 1 indicate many strain-specific genes; high counts at the maximum x value indicate a large shared core genome.





The distribution of gene annotations shows a high number of genes shared across all strains and many genes unique to individual strains. Genes present in all strains likely represent core functions essential for survival, while strain-specific genes suggest functional diversity and adaptation to different environmental conditions.
Mal strains show a larger proportion of genes shared across all or most strains, indicating a more conserved core genome. In contrast, Dimeth strains have a higher proportion of genes unique to individual strains and fewer genes shared across all strains, suggesting greater genomic variability.



Gene annotation sharing across strain types

# Classify annotations as unique, partially shared, or shared across all types
n_types_total <- df |>
  distinct(StrainType) |>
  nrow()

df |>
  filter(!is.na(Description), Description != "") |>
  distinct(StrainType, Description) |>
  add_count(Description, name = "n_types") |>
  mutate(
    sharing_type = case_when(
      n_types == 1            ~ "Unique",
      n_types == n_types_total ~ "Shared (All)",
      TRUE                    ~ "Partial"
    )
  ) |>
  count(StrainType, sharing_type) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = StrainType, y = n, fill = sharing_type)) +
  geom_col(position = "fill") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = colors_van_likes[c(1, 5, 2)]) +
  labs(
    title   = "Gene annotation sharing across strain types",
    x       = "Strain type",
    y       = "Proportion of gene annotations",
    fill    = "Sharing type",
    caption = paste(
      "Unique: found in 1 strain type only.",
      "Shared (All): present in all 4 strain types.",
      "Partial: present in 2–3 strain types.",
      sep = " "
    )
  ) +
  theme_classic()







Figure 3: Proportional stacked bar chart showing what fraction of each strain type’s gene annotations are unique to that type, shared with all four types, or partially shared with 2–3 types.





Gene annotations were compared based on functional descriptions, allowing identification of shared and unique functional categories across strain types.



Functional composition by strain type

# Summarise functional category proportions at the strain-type level
df |>
  mutate(Category = categorize_function(Description)) |>
  count(StrainType, Category) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = StrainType, y = n, fill = Category)) +
  geom_col(position = "fill") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = colors_van_likes) +
  labs(
    title   = "Functional composition by strain type",
    x       = "Strain type",
    y       = "Proportion of genes",
    fill    = "Functional category",
    caption = "Source: OmicsBox functional annotation tables"
  ) +
  theme_classic()







Figure 4: Proportional stacked bar chart of functional gene categories aggregated by strain type, allowing comparison of overall functional composition between Mal, Dimeth, Chlorp, and Diaz.





Functional proportions are broadly similar across strain types, with Transport and Metabolism dominating in all groups. Minor differences in Stress/Defense and Regulation proportions may reflect adaptation to distinct ecological niches.

title: Portfolio Part 1
text:
1. Identify your data source. The primary data sources are derived from an ongoing comparative genomic analysis of pesticide-degrading bacteria. The dataset consists of two outputs generated from bioinformatics pipelines:

Gene prediction data generated via Prodigal, provided in .fna (FASTA nucleic acid) format.
Functional annotation data generated via OmicsBox, which will be exported as a CSV file.

2. Describe your data, including variables and data types. The data will be organized from two separate files:

Sequence Dataset: Contains Gene_ID (character string) and raw nucleotide Sequence (character string).
Annotation Dataset: Contains Gene_ID (character string), Enzyme_Code or GO_Terms (character string), and Description (character string indicating specific biological functions). Once combined and processed, the final dataset will primarily utilize Gene_ID (character), GC_Content (numeric percentage), and Gene_Category (factor: Pesticide-Degrading vs. Core Genome).

3. Identify the research questions you want to answer. Since human-made pesticides are relatively recent in evolutionary history, are the pesticide-degrading genes in these bacteria likely acquired via Horizontal Gene Transfer (HGT) rather than evolving from ancestral genes? Can we use compositional bias—specifically, significant deviations in GC content compared to the rest of the genome—as a visual screening tool to identify these HGT candidates?



1. What do you want your final visualizations to look like? I plan to create two main visualizations:

An overlaid density plot (geom_density) showing the distribution of GC percentages.
A side-by-side boxplot (geom_boxplot) to compare the median and spread of the GC percentages between the two groups.

2. What do you want to highlight on your final visualizations in order to answer your research questions? How do you plan to do that? I want to highlight the compositional bias (deviation in GC%) of the pesticide-degrading genes compared to the core genome. I plan to do this by overlaying the density curve of the pesticide-degrading genes in a distinct, high-contrast color over the broader, background density curve of the core genome.
3. What is missing from your data or would need to change in your data to create these visualizations? The raw .fna file only provides the raw DNA letter sequences. The actual GC percentage is missing and needs to be mathematically calculated and added as a new numeric column. Additionally, the sequence data and the functional annotation data exist in two separate files and must be merged into a single dataframe to group the visualizations by gene function.



Do you need to reformat any variables into different types? YES. The specific gene categories derived from the OmicsBox descriptions need to be reformatted as factor variables (e.g., “Target HGT Candidate” vs “Background Genome”) so that ggplot2 can correctly assign distinct colors and groupings.
Do you need to deal with any missing data, especially missing data coded other than NA? YES. Some genes in the OmicsBox output may lack specific functional descriptions or GO terms (often left as blank strings rather than NA). I will need to recode these empty strings to NA or “Hypothetical Protein” to maintain a clean dataset.
Do you need to filter your data? How? YES. I will need to filter out extremely short sequences (e.g., sequences < 100 base pairs). Very short sequences can produce artificially skewed and extreme GC percentages, which would introduce noise into the distribution plots.
Do you need to create any new variables? What variables? How? YES. I need to create a GC_Content variable. This will be done by calculating the proportion of ‘G’ and ‘C’ characters within the total length of the raw Sequence string. I also need to create a binary categorical variable (Is_Target) using ifelse() and grepl() to flag genes whose descriptions contain pesticide-degrading keywords.
Do you need to add new data (join) to your data? What data? How? YES. I need to join the calculated GC content dataset with the OmicsBox functional annotation dataset. I will use a left_join() or inner_join() from the dplyr package, matching the rows by their shared Gene_ID column.
Are there any variables you can exclude from your data? YES. I will use select() to exclude unnecessary columns exported from OmicsBox, such as sequence lengths, e-values, and redundant database mapping IDs, keeping only the IDs and functional descriptions needed for the plots.
